design_philosophy.core_loop: Linear stage progression through beat-em-up combat, defeating waves of enemies and mini-bosses to advance
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management (health/power-ups) vs. incoming enemy waves; difficulty spikes between regional versions
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as Kenshiro from the manga, using martial arts techniques to progress through post-apocalyptic stages
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Regional difficulty balancing: Japanese version includes random projectile attacks and stricter mini-boss hit requirements; US version significantly eased progression by removing projectiles and relaxing enemy patterns. Suggests design philosophy shifted between markets.

difficulty.continues_system: Regional difference: Japanese version forces continue to Stage 4; US version continues from current stage
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Hidden cheat codes allow maximum power-up at start (Hold A + Start) and level select (Right presses + Hold B + Start), suggesting developer acknowledgment of difficulty tuning needs

progression.upgrade_system: Power-up pickups during stages (renamed from 'abeshi' and 'hidebu' in Japanese to white/black stars in US version)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Sequential stage progression; hidden level select cheat available
replayability_hooks: Hidden level select for non-linear progression
Regional version differences create alternative play experiences

world_building_techniques: Character appearances (NPCs removed in US version)
Ending credits with character reveals (Lui picture in Japanese version only)

notable_bosses: Mini-bosses with region-specific hit requirements (head-targeting in Japanese version)

danger_signals: Random projectile attacks from screen sides (Japanese version only)
environmental_storytelling: NPC encounters at stage endings (Japanese version feature)
Character sprite changes between versions (Kenshiro black→blue recolor)

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Fist of the North Star (NES)
